trajectory_summary: The call opened with Herbert seizing the agenda on cost and never fully releasing it. Meg booked the appointment but did so by outlasting Herbert's resistance rather than resolving it — the cost question was deflected four times, scope crept into net metering and on-peak/off-peak explanations, and the button-up was thin on the structural anchors that protect show rate. The booking is real but fragile: Herbert is transactionally cooperative, not bought in.
outcome_dispute: The call ends with a booked appointment (Friday the 6th at 2:30 PM), so a 'booked' tag would be accurate. However, the booking quality is low — no spouse/co-owner check, no 'at your computer' anchor, no 'is that fair' handshake, and Herbert's cost question was never resolved. The appointment is structurally vulnerable to no-show or disqualification at the closer stage.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Objection inversion on cost — deploying the bill as the answer
primary_coaching_focus.quote: just first thing All I wanna know is what's the what's the, you know, general range of cost for something like that.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Herbert raised the cost question four times and Meg deflected it four times with process rationale. The $700 bill he volunteered at 01:42 was the answer to his own question — Meg never used it. This is the call's central failure: the bill was captured but never flipped, and every subsequent deflection compounded Herbert's suspicion. Until Meg can deploy the inversion ('your current bill is the cost we're replacing'), she will keep generating the runaround dynamic on cost-first customers.

key_moments.quote: just first thing All I wanna know is what's the what's the, you know, general range of cost for something like that.
key_moments.stewart_read: Herbert hands Meg a cost objection in the first 30 seconds — the highest-leverage inversion opportunity in the call. Meg's response ('let me explain how the program works') is a safe pivot to the bill-swap frame, but it doesn't use Herbert's own framing as the lever. The inversion available here: his current bill IS the cost — the question is whether he keeps paying Rappahannock or replaces it with something cheaper. Reactive read holds: Meg missed the flip and defaulted to program explanation.

key_moments.quote: No. I've looked into it before. As a matter of fact, I currently have a solar panel on the roof, but it only it it's only for hot water, not for electricity.
key_moments.stewart_read: Herbert discloses an existing solar installation. The schema orphaned_flow protocol requires the rep to work through a defined sequence: own vs. lease, original company, panel size, and whether the system is working. Meg's response is 'Just putting that in my notes here' and an immediate pivot to bill collection. None of the four orphaned_flow questions were asked. The hot-water-only distinction means this is likely not a hard DQ, but the protocol still requires the check — Meg skipped it entirely.

key_moments.quote: You're just giving me a runaround right now. I feel like I'm getting a runaround for, you know, for another reason.
key_moments.stewart_read: Herbert's 'another reason' phrasing is a scam-anxiety signal — he's not just impatient, he's questioning the legitimacy of the bill-photo request. The schema scam_anxiety_signal directive calls for an appointment-reframe that anchors the call as customer-initiated. Meg's response ('Yeah. So the reason is because, like like I mentioned earlier...') re-explains the process rationale, which is exactly the wrong move — it defends the procedure rather than addressing the trust concern. The empathy miss here compounds the cost-question deflection pattern.

key_moments.quote: What happens at nighttime when there's no sun or it's cloudy and there's no sun for several days like now.
key_moments.stewart_read: Herbert's net metering question is a technical question that belongs to the closer. Meg's response is a 90-second explanation of net metering mechanics, credits, and energy banking — sustained scope creep into closer-tier content. The explanation doesn't resolve Herbert's skepticism (he pushes back at 12:08: 'You don't get enough credits. You still gotta pay right behind it.') and leads directly to his fourth cost-question at 12:45. The scope creep created more objections than it resolved.
